title: EIP-8075: Adaptive state cost to cap growth & scale L1
description: Introduces an EIP-4844-style fee market for state creation, dynamically adjusting the gas cost per state byte to target 250 MiB/day of state growth. State creation and regular gas receive separate block limits, preventing state gas from crowding out compute and other operations. A normalized aggregate of both is used when updating the base fee, preserving unified transaction pricing.
author: Anders Elowsson (@anderselowsson), Francesco D'Amato (@fradamt), Maria Silva (@misilva73)
laymanDescription: Currently, growing the Ethereum gas limit also grows the blockchain's stored data at an unsustainable rate. This proposal creates a separate, self-adjusting price for adding new data to the state, targeting a safe daily growth rate. By giving state its own budget, Ethereum can raise throughput for regular transactions without database bloat.

endUsers.description: Transactions that create new state (accounts, storage slots) may cost more or less over time as the adaptive mechanism adjusts, but wallets handle this automatically.

appDevs.description: Smart contract developers should minimize unnecessary state creation. Existing contracts work unchanged, but gas estimates for state-heavy operations will fluctuate.

walletDevs.description: Wallets must fetch excess_state_bytes to compute state_gas_per_byte and add a margin to gas limits for state-heavy transactions, similar to blob fee handling.

toolingInfra.description: Block explorers and gas estimators must track new header fields (state_bytes_created, state_bytes_cleared, excess_state_bytes) and the separate state gas dimension.

layer2s.description: No direct impact on L2 protocols. L2s posting state roots or data to L1 may see fluctuating costs for storage-set operations under the adaptive pricing.

stakersNodes.description: Node operators benefit from capped state growth, keeping database sizes manageable and sustaining long-term performance as the gas limit increases.

clClients.description: Minimal direct impact. Consensus clients may need to handle the new header fields during block validation but core CL logic is unaffected.

elClients.description: Significant implementation work: separate gas accounting for regular and state gas, new header fields, EIP-4844-style excess tracking, and modified base fee calculation.

benefits: Precisely caps state growth at a sustainable target regardless of gas limit increases.
Unlocks 50-300% more non-state throughput by exempting state from the regular gas limit.
Uses a proven EIP-4844-style mechanism, keeping the existing transaction format intact.
Smoothly adjusts state cost between blocks, limiting price drift to at most 0.1% per block.
tradeoffs: Wallets must estimate state gas margins, adding complexity to gas limit calculation.
Tight gas limits on state-heavy transactions risk failure if state_gas_per_byte drifts between submission and execution.
Introduces a new gas dimension, increasing protocol and tooling complexity.

scaleL1.description: Directly enables L1 scaling by decoupling state cost from the block gas limit, allowing significant throughput increases without proportional state bloat.
